g.data-narration: Welcome to Logical Implication and Rules of Inference - the foundation of mathematical reasoning and proof construction.
g.text: Logical Implication and Rules of Inference

g.data-narration: Logical implication and rules of inference form the foundation for mathematical reasoning, algorithms, and software verification.
g.text: • Foundation for mathematical proofs and algorithm design
• Essential for database queries, circuit design, and verification
• Appears consistently in competitive exams and interviews

g.data-narration: We will explore logical implication p→q, eight rules of inference, common fallacies, and formal proof construction.
g.text: 1. Logical Implication (p → q) and Truth Table
3. Eight Rules of Inference with Examples

g.data-narration: Logical implication p→q is false only when p is true and q is false. It is equivalent to ¬p ∨ q.
g.text: Definition: "If p then q" - false only when p=T and q=F

g.data-narration: The contrapositive ¬q→¬p is logically equivalent to the original, while converse and inverse are not equivalent.
A tautology is always true, contradiction is always false, and contingency depends on truth values.
A valid argument guarantees true conclusion when all premises are true. It is about logical structure, not truth.
g.text: An argument is valid if whenever ALL premises are true, the CONCLUSION must also be true.
Validity is about structure, not whether premises are actually true!

g.data-narration: Modus Ponens: From p→q and p, conclude q. Example: If it rains, ground gets wet. It rains. Therefore wet.
Modus Tollens: From p→q and ¬q, conclude ¬p. Example: If it rains, ground wet. Ground not wet. Therefore not raining.
Hypothetical Syllogism: From p→q and q→r, conclude p→r. Chain implications together logically.
Disjunctive Syllogism: From p∨q and ¬p, conclude q. Eliminate one option to find the other.
Addition: p→p∨q. Simplification: p∧q→p. Conjunction: p,q→p∧q. Basic building blocks for proofs.
Resolution: p∨q, ¬p∨r ⊢ q∨r. Also learn common fallacies: affirming consequent and denying antecedent.
Let's construct a formal proof: Given (p∧q)→r, p, q; prove r using conjunction and modus ponens.
g.text: Premises: (p ∧ q) → r, p, q | Conclusion: r

g.data-narration: Here are all eight rules of inference with their premise-conclusion patterns for quick reference.
Remember: Implication is false only when antecedent true and consequent false. Contrapositive is equivalent. Practice proofs!
g.text: p→q is false only when p=T, q=F | Contrapositive ≡ Original | Practice identifying rules in proofs!
